senses.definition: Pieces of land or property held in complete private ownership, not owing any duty to a lord or higher authority, in old European law.
examples: In medieval times, only a few nobles controlled allods that were free from feudal duties.
The charter records several allods granted to the monastery by local landowners.

senses.definition: An older or less common form of the word allod, meaning land held in full private ownership without feudal duties.
examples: The document refers to an allody transferred to the abbey in the twelfth century.
Only one small allody remained outside the control of the local duke.

senses.definition: A medical condition in which a person feels pain from things that do not normally cause pain, such as a light touch.
examples: Patients with allodynia may find even gentle clothing on the skin extremely painful.
The doctor explained that her headaches were linked to allodynia after nerve damage.

senses.definition: Plural of allodynia; several cases or types of pain caused by normally non-painful stimuli.
examples: The study compared different allodynias in patients with diabetic neuropathy.
Researchers identified three main allodynias affecting the volunteers' skin.

senses.definition: A rare rhetorical device in which one grammatical person (such as I, you, or he) is used instead of another.
examples: The orator used alloeosis to speak of himself in the third person for effect.
In classical rhetoric, alloeosis can make an argument sound more objective.

senses.definition: A highly specialized linguistic term for a change or irregularity in word endings or inflection compared with a usual pattern.
examples: The grammarian classified the unusual plural as an instance of alloeostropha.
Alloeostropha in ancient texts can create problems for modern editors.

senses.definition: Showing or involving change from one state or form to another.
examples: The author describes an alloeotic shift in the hero's personality.
Some biologists have used the term alloeotic for certain developmental changes.

senses.definition: Relating to sexual desire that is directed toward someone other than oneself.
examples: The study contrasted autoerotic and alloerotic fantasies in adults.
Alloerotic behavior is shaped by both biology and culture.

senses.definition: Sexual interest or arousal that is focused on another person rather than on oneself.
examples: Some theories see alloerotism as a basic feature of human bonding.
The therapist discussed how trauma had affected his alloerotism.

senses.definition: Plural of allogamy; different cases or types of cross-fertilization between separate individuals of the same species.
examples: The botanist compared several allogamies observed in wild plant populations.
These allogamies help maintain genetic diversity in the species.

senses.definition: Reproducing by cross-fertilization between different individuals of the same species.
examples: Many wild grasses are allogamous and depend on wind for pollination.
Allogamous species often show high genetic variation.

senses.definition: Cross-fertilization between different individuals of the same species, especially in plants.
examples: Allogamy increases genetic variation within a plant population.
The crop shows a high rate of allogamy under field conditions.

senses.definition: A substance, usually a tissue or antigen, that comes from a genetically different individual of the same species and can cause an immune reaction.
examples: The transplanted organ contained an allogene that triggered rejection.
Researchers isolated the allogene responsible for the strong response.

senses.definition: Relating to cells, tissues, or organs taken from a genetically different person of the same species.
examples: The patient received an allogeneic bone marrow transplant from a sibling.
Allogeneic transplants require strong drugs to prevent rejection.

senses.definition: The quality of being genetically different from another individual of the same species.
examples: Allogeneity between donor and recipient increased the risk of graft rejection.
The study measured the degree of allogeneity in the population sample.

senses.definition: Having a different origin or being of a different kind, especially genetically different within the same species.
examples: Allogeneous tissue can stimulate a strong immune response.
The sample contained both allogeneous and autologous cell lines.

senses.definition: Arising from or produced by factors that come from outside the system or area being considered; in medicine, having cells or tissues from another genetically different individual of the same species.
examples: The lake's allogenic sediments were carried in by the river.
The patient underwent allogenic stem cell transplantation.

senses.definition: In a way that involves cells, tissues, or influences coming from a genetically different individual of the same species, or from outside the system.
examples: The mice were allogenically transplanted with donor marrow.
Sediment was allogenically supplied from the surrounding highlands.

senses.definition: Tissue or an organ taken from one person and transplanted into another person of the same species who is not genetically identical.
examples: The surgeon used a bone allograft to repair the patient's spine.
Allograft rejection remains a major challenge after transplantation.

senses.definition: Transplanted tissue or an organ from one person to another genetically different person of the same species.
examples: The damaged ligament was allografted using donor tissue.
Skin was allografted onto the burn area to speed healing.

senses.definition: Describing tissue or an organ that has been transplanted from a genetically different donor of the same species.
examples: The allografted kidney functioned well after surgery.
Allografted corneas can restore sight to blind patients.

senses.definition: The act or process of transplanting tissue or an organ from one person to another genetically different person of the same species.
examples: Allografting has become routine in many orthopedic procedures.
The team discussed the risks of infection associated with allografting.

senses.definition: Plural of allograft; tissues or organs taken from one person and transplanted into other genetically different people of the same species.
examples: Allografts are widely used in bone and ligament repair surgeries.
The hospital stores allografts from screened donors in a tissue bank.

senses.definition: A different written form of the same letter, word, or sign, such as a printed letter compared with a handwritten one.
examples: The researchers trained software to recognize various allographs of each letter.
Calligraphers study many allographs of the Latin alphabet.

senses.definition: A document written by one person but signed in the name of another.
examples: The contract was an allograph prepared by the notary.
Historical archives contain many allographs written by professional scribes.

senses.definition: Relating to different written forms of the same letter, word, or sign.
examples: Allographic variation can confuse automatic handwriting recognition.
The font set covers allographic forms used in early manuscripts.

senses.definition: Plural of allograph; different written forms of the same letter, word, or sign.
examples: Children must learn to recognize allographs such as printed and cursive letters.
The study grouped the allographs into families based on shape.

senses.definition: Relating to an immune response in which a person’s body reacts against cells or tissues from another person of the same species.
examples: Alloimmune reactions can damage transplanted organs.
The baby developed an alloimmune condition due to maternal antibodies.

senses.definition: Development that takes a course different from the usual pattern, sometimes producing an abnormal form.
examples: The larvae showed alloiogenesis after exposure to the chemical.
Alloiogenesis in embryos may result in serious malformations.

senses.definition: Relating to changes in the growth rate of one part of an organism compared with another part or with the whole body.
examples: The species shows alloiometric growth of its limbs.
Alloiometric patterns can reveal how body shape evolves.

senses.definition: The study of how the growth of one part of an organism relates to the growth of another part or to the whole body.
examples: Alloiometry helps explain why young animals have relatively large heads.
The researcher specializes in fish alloiometry and body shape.

senses.definition: Having a verse or stanza form that differs from the usual pattern in a poem or song.
examples: The hymn includes an alloiostrophos stanza in the middle.
Editors debated how to print the alloiostrophos lines.

senses.definition: A chemical compound that has the same proportions of elements as another compound but a different total molecular weight.
examples: The mineral has an alloisomer with a lower molecular mass.
Chemists identified an alloisomer of the original substance.

senses.definition: Describing compounds that have the same relative proportions of elements but different overall molecular weights.
examples: Alloisomeric compounds can have distinct physical properties.
The crystals exhibit an alloisomeric relationship.

senses.definition: The condition of compounds having the same relative proportions of elements but different molecular weights.
examples: Alloisomerism was proposed to explain the mineral's puzzling analyses.
The phenomenon of alloisomerism complicates the classification of some salts.

senses.definition: Movement that is triggered by a stimulus but not directed toward or away from the source of the stimulus.
examples: The tiny crustaceans display allokinesis when light levels change.
Allokinesis contrasts with directed movement such as taxis.

senses.definition: Relating to or showing movement that is caused by a stimulus but not aimed toward or away from it.
examples: The species' allokinetic response increases its chance of finding food.
Allokinetic behavior is common in simple organisms.

senses.definition: Showing a statistical distribution with a kurtosis that differs from the normal distribution.
examples: The data are clearly allokurtic and not normally distributed.
Allokurtic variables may require special statistical methods.

senses.definition: A speech disorder in which a person uses the wrong words or mixes languages while speaking.
examples: Allolalia can appear after certain kinds of brain injury.
The patient's allolalia made conversation difficult to follow.

senses.definition: Relating to or affected by allolalia, especially in speech that mixes or misuses words.
examples: Her allolalic utterances switched suddenly between languages.
The clinician noted several allolalic features in his speech.

senses.definition: Relating to or showing allomerism, in which compounds have the same proportions of elements but different molecular weights.
examples: Allomeric minerals can be difficult to tell apart visually.
The substance has two allomeric forms with different densities.

senses.definition: A type of isomerism where substances have the same proportions of elements but different molecular weights.
examples: Allomerism may account for the differing analyses of the samples.
The textbook gives classic examples of allomerism in mineral salts.

senses.definition: Plural of allomerism; different cases in which substances share the same element proportions but differ in molecular weight.
examples: Several allomerisms have been reported in the literature.
The mineral family shows multiple allomerisms.

senses.definition: The process by which one allomer changes into another or is produced.
examples: Heating may cause allomerization of the compound.
The mechanism of allomerization remains unclear.

senses.definition: To change a compound into an allomeric form or to undergo such a change.
examples: The salt can allomerize slowly at room temperature.
When heated, the crystals allomerize into a denser form.

senses.definition: Changed into an allomeric form or having undergone allomerization.
examples: The compound allomerized during long storage.
Allomerized samples showed different melting points.

senses.definition: Undergoing or causing allomerization, changing into an allomeric form.
examples: The allomerizing solution slowly turned cloudy.
While allomerizing, the compound releases heat.

senses.definition: Having the same proportions of elements as another compound but a different molecular weight.
examples: Two allomerous minerals may look nearly identical.
The salt occurs in several allomerous forms.

senses.definition: Relating to how different parts of an organism grow at different rates, changing body shape as size changes.
examples: Allometric growth explains why babies have relatively large heads.
The study analyzed allometric relationships between body mass and brain size.

senses.definition: Plural of allometry; different patterns or studies of how body parts change size in relation to overall size.
examples: The book reviews various allometries in mammalian skulls.
Comparative allometries reveal how species differ in body shape.

senses.definition: The study of how the size of one part of an organism changes in relation to the size of another part or to the whole body.
examples: Allometry helps explain the shapes of animals as they grow.
He specializes in the allometry of tree branches and trunks.

senses.definition: A chemical produced by one species that affects the behavior or biology of another species in a way that benefits the producer.
examples: The plant releases an allomone that repels insects.
Many predators use allomones to attract or confuse their prey.
